statement: EU–Norway merchandise trade dwarfs EU–Iceland trade by a factor of roughly 19:1. In 2024, EU exports to Norway totalled €62.9 billion and EU imports from Norway €96.8 billion, yielding a €33.9 billion EU deficit — driven largely by Norwegian oil and gas exports. By contrast, EU–Iceland total trade was €8.6 billion with a near-balanced position. Norway's trade with the EU surged during the 2022 energy crisis, with EU imports from Norway reaching €161.2 billion that year.
caveats: Norway's trade profile is dominated by petroleum exports, making direct comparisons with Iceland's trade structure misleading. The 2022 spike in EU imports from Norway reflected energy prices, not underlying economic integration.

related_entries.statement: Total merchandise trade between the EU and Iceland reached approximately €8.6 billion in 2024, comprising €4.5 billion in EU exports to Iceland and €4.1 billion in EU imports from Iceland. This represents significant growth from €3.7 billion in total trade in 2010, more than doubling over 14 years. The EU maintained a modest trade surplus with Iceland in 2024 of €387 million, though the balance fluctuates year to year — Iceland ran a small deficit with the EU in 2021–2022.

The European Union is Iceland's largest trading partner. In 2024, approximately 50% of Iceland's goods exports and 60% of goods imports by value were with EU member states. Marine products (primarily fish) constitute the largest single export category to the EU.
